title: Dornröschen
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 50. Text from Wikisource („Dornröschen (1857)“, twice proofread against the original). Fresh translation made directly from the German; 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The fiftieth tale (KHM 50), the Grimms’ Sleeping Beauty. A long-wished-for princess is cursed by a slighted wise woman to prick her finger and die, softened to a hundred-year sleep that swallows the whole castle in a hedge of thorns, until the appointed day brings the prince through. Spelling follows 1857, with forms such as gieng, ward, Thür and funfzehn.

sentences.t: Vor Zeiten war ein König und eine Königin, die sprachen jeden Tag „ach, wenn wir doch ein Kind hätten!“ und kriegten immer keins.
sentences.l: In former times there was a king and a queen, who said every day, “oh, if only we had a child!” and never got any.
sentences.i: In days of old there lived a king and a queen who said every day, “Oh, if only we had a child!”—and yet never had one.
sentences.n: “Vor Zeiten” is a set fairy-tale opening, ‘in olden times.’ “kriegten” is the colloquial preterite of kriegen (‘to get’), for bekamen; “keins” = kein + es, ‘none / not any.’

sentences.t: Da trug sich zu, als die Königin einmal im Bade saß, daß ein Frosch aus dem Wasser ans Land kroch und zu ihr sprach, „dein Wunsch wird erfüllt werden, ehe ein Jahr vergeht, wirst du eine Tochter zur Welt bringen.“
sentences.l: Then it came to pass, as the queen was once sitting in the bath, that a frog crept out of the water onto the land and said to her, “your wish will be fulfilled; before a year passes, you will bring a daughter into the world.”
sentences.i: Now it came to pass, as the queen sat one day in her bath, that a frog crept out of the water onto the land and said to her, “Your wish shall be granted; before a year has gone by, you shall bring a daughter into the world.”
sentences.n: “trug sich zu” is the separable reflexive zutragen (‘to come to pass, happen’). “wird erfüllt werden” is future passive: ‘will be fulfilled.’ “zur Welt bringen” (lit. ‘to bring to the world’) is the fixed idiom ‘to give birth to.’

sentences.t: Was der Frosch gesagt hatte, das geschah, und die Königin gebar ein Mädchen, das war so schön, daß der König vor Freude sich nicht zu lassen wußte und ein großes Fest anstellte.
sentences.l: What the frog had said, that happened, and the queen bore a girl who was so beautiful that the king, for joy, did not know how to contain himself, and arranged a great feast.
sentences.i: What the frog had foretold came true, and the queen bore a girl so beautiful that the king, out of sheer joy, did not know how to contain himself, and held a great feast.
sentences.n: “sich nicht zu lassen wissen” is an idiom, ‘not to know what to do with oneself’ (here from joy). “vor Freude” — vor + dative gives the cause of an emotion, ‘out of joy.’ “anstellte” is separable anstellen, here ‘to arrange, put on.’

sentences.t: Er ladete nicht blos seine Verwandte, Freunde und Bekannte, sondern auch die weisen Frauen dazu ein, damit sie dem Kind hold und gewogen wären.
sentences.l: He invited not merely his relatives, friends and acquaintances, but also the wise women to it, so that they might be kindly and favourably disposed toward the child.
sentences.i: He invited not only his kinsfolk, friends and acquaintances, but the wise women as well, so that they might be gracious and well-disposed toward the child.
sentences.n: “ladete ... ein” is the separable einladen (‘to invite’), here with the older weak preterite ladete. “nicht blos ... sondern auch” = ‘not only ... but also’ (blos is the 1857 spelling of bloß). “dem Kind hold” — hold takes the dative: ‘gracious to the child.’

sentences.t: Es waren ihrer dreizehn in seinem Reiche, weil er aber nur zwölf goldene Teller hatte, von welchen sie essen sollten, so mußte eine von ihnen daheim bleiben.
sentences.l: There were thirteen of them in his realm, but because he had only twelve golden plates, from which they were to eat, one of them had to stay at home.
sentences.i: There were thirteen of them in his realm, but as he had only twelve golden plates for them to eat from, one of them had to stay behind at home.
sentences.n: “ihrer dreizehn” is an old partitive genitive: ‘thirteen of them.’ Note the “weil ... aber ... so” frame, where “so” resumes the main clause after the subordinate one.

sentences.t: Das Fest ward mit aller Pracht gefeiert, und als es zu Ende war, beschenkten die weisen Frauen das Kind mit ihren Wundergaben: die eine mit Tugend, die andere mit Schönheit, die dritte mit Reichthum, und so mit allem, was auf der Welt zu wünschen ist.
sentences.l: The feast was celebrated with all splendour, and when it was at an end, the wise women bestowed on the child their magic gifts: the one with virtue, the other with beauty, the third with riches, and so with everything that is to be wished for in the world.
sentences.i: The feast was held with all magnificence, and when it drew to a close, the wise women endowed the child with their wondrous gifts: one with virtue, another with beauty, a third with riches, and so with all that one could wish for in the world.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘was, became’), for modern wurde — you meet it throughout Grimm. “beschenken mit” = ‘to present/endow with.’

sentences.t: Als elfe ihre Sprüche eben gethan hatten, trat plötzlich die dreizehnte herein.
sentences.l: When eleven of them had just spoken their sayings, suddenly the thirteenth stepped in.
sentences.i: When eleven of them had just pronounced their blessings, the thirteenth suddenly walked in.
sentences.n: “elfe” is an older form of elf (‘eleven,’ here ‘eleven of them’). “gethan” is the 1857 spelling of getan (past part. of thun/tun). “trat ... herein” is separable hereintreten, ‘to step in.’

sentences.t: Sie wollte sich dafür rächen daß sie nicht eingeladen war, und ohne jemand zu grüßen oder nur anzusehen, rief sie mit lauter Stimme „die Königstochter soll sich in ihrem funfzehnten Jahr an einer Spindel stechen und todt hinfallen.“
sentences.l: She wanted to avenge herself for the fact that she had not been invited, and without greeting anyone or even so much as looking at them, she called out with a loud voice, “the king’s daughter shall, in her fifteenth year, prick herself on a spindle and fall down dead.”
sentences.i: She meant to take revenge for not having been invited, and without greeting anyone or so much as glancing at them, she cried out in a loud voice, “In her fifteenth year the king’s daughter shall prick herself on a spindle and fall down dead.”
sentences.n: “sich rächen für” = ‘to avenge oneself for.’ “dafür ... daß” is a split correlative: ‘for the fact that.’ “nur anzusehen” — nur here means ‘even, so much as’; “anzusehen” is zu + ansehen (separable an-). “funfzehnten” and “todt” are 1857 spellings of fünfzehnten and tot.

sentences.t: Und ohne ein Wort weiter zu sprechen kehrte sie sich um und verließ den Saal.
sentences.l: And without speaking a word further, she turned herself round and left the hall.
sentences.n: “kehrte sich um” is the separable reflexive sich umkehren, ‘to turn round.’ “ohne ... zu sprechen” = ‘without speaking.’

sentences.t: Alle waren erschrocken, da trat die zwölfte hervor, die ihren Wunsch noch übrig hatte und weil sie den bösen Spruch nicht aufheben, sondern nur ihn mildern konnte, so sagte sie „es soll aber kein Tod sein, sondern ein hundertjähriger tiefer Schlaf, in welchen die Königstochter fällt.“
sentences.l: All were terrified; then the twelfth stepped forth, who still had her wish left over, and because she could not undo the evil pronouncement, but only soften it, she said, “but it shall not be death, rather a hundred-year-long deep sleep, into which the king’s daughter falls.”
sentences.i: All were terrified; then the twelfth came forward, who still had her wish in reserve, and since she could not lift the evil spell but only soften it, she said, “Yet it shall not be death, but a hundred-year-long deep sleep, into which the king’s daughter shall fall.”
sentences.n: “noch übrig hatte” — übrig haben, ‘to have left over, in reserve.’ “aufheben” here means ‘to annul, revoke’ (a spell). Note “nicht ... sondern” twice: ‘not X but Y.’

sentences.t: Der König, der sein liebes Kind vor dem Unglück gern bewahren wollte, ließ den Befehl ausgehen, daß alle Spindeln im ganzen Königreiche sollten verbrannt werden.
sentences.l: The king, who gladly wanted to preserve his dear child from the misfortune, let the command go out that all spindles in the whole kingdom should be burned.
sentences.i: The king, wishing dearly to guard his beloved child from this misfortune, issued a command that all the spindles in the whole kingdom should be burned.
sentences.n: “ließ den Befehl ausgehen” — lassen + infinitive of ausgehen (separable), ‘to have an order go out, to issue an order.’ “vor dem Unglück bewahren” — bewahren vor + dative, ‘to guard from.’ “verbrannt werden” is passive, ‘to be burned.’

sentences.t: An dem Mädchen aber wurden die Gaben der weisen Frauen sämmtlich erfüllt, denn es war so schön, sittsam, freundlich und verständig, daß es jedermann, der es ansah, lieb haben mußte.
sentences.l: In the girl, however, the gifts of the wise women were all fulfilled, for she was so beautiful, modest, friendly and sensible that everyone who looked at her could not help but hold her dear.
sentences.i: In the maiden, however, the wise women’s gifts were all fulfilled, for she was so beautiful, modest, gentle and sensible that everyone who saw her could not help loving her.
sentences.n: “sämmtlich” (1857 spelling of sämtlich) = ‘all, in their entirety.’ “lieb haben” = ‘to be fond of, to love’; “mußte” here is ‘could not but,’ obligation of feeling. Note the neuter “es” for das Mädchen (grammatical gender, not sense).

sentences.t: Es geschah, daß an dem Tage, wo es gerade funfzehn Jahr alt ward, der König und die Königin nicht zu Haus waren, und das Mädchen ganz allein im Schloß zurückblieb.
sentences.l: It happened that on the day when she had just turned fifteen years old, the king and the queen were not at home, and the girl remained behind quite alone in the castle.
sentences.i: It happened that on the very day she turned fifteen, the king and queen were not at home, and the girl was left all alone in the castle.
sentences.n: “funfzehn Jahr alt ward” — ‘became fifteen years old,’ with ward the archaic preterite of werden. “zurückblieb” is separable zurückbleiben, ‘to stay behind.’

sentences.t: Da gieng es aller Orten herum, besah Stuben und Kammern, wie es Lust hatte, und kam endlich auch an einen alten Thurm.
sentences.l: Then she went about all places, looked over rooms and chambers, as she had a mind to, and at last came also to an old tower.
sentences.i: So she wandered everywhere, examining rooms and chambers as she pleased, and came at last to an old tower.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (preterite of gehen); you meet gieng, fieng, anfieng throughout this edition. “aller Orten” is a fixed old phrase, ‘everywhere.’ “wie es Lust hatte” = ‘as she pleased.’ “Thurm” keeps the old -h- spelling.

sentences.t: Es stieg die enge Wendeltreppe hinauf, und gelangte zu einer kleinen Thüre.
sentences.l: She climbed the narrow winding staircase up, and reached a little door.
sentences.i: She climbed up the narrow spiral staircase and came to a little door.
sentences.n: “stieg ... hinauf” is separable hinaufsteigen, ‘to climb up.’ “Thüre” is the 1857 spelling of Türe/Tür.

sentences.t: In dem Schloß steckte ein verrosteter Schlüssel, und als es umdrehte, sprang die Thüre auf, und saß da in einem kleinen Stübchen eine alte Frau mit einer Spindel und spann emsig ihren Flachs.
sentences.l: In the lock stuck a rusty key, and when she turned it, the door sprang open, and there sat in a little room an old woman with a spindle, spinning her flax busily.
sentences.i: A rusty key was in the lock, and when she turned it, the door sprang open, and there in a little room sat an old woman with a spindle, busily spinning her flax.
sentences.n: “Schloß” here means ‘lock’ (also ‘castle’ elsewhere — same word). “sprang ... auf” is separable aufspringen, ‘to spring open.’ “saß da” inverts verb before subject after the “und,” a common narrative rhythm.

sentences.t: „Guten Tag, du altes Mütterchen,“ sprach die Königstochter, „was machst du da?“
sentences.l: “Good day, you old little mother,” said the king’s daughter, “what are you doing there?”
sentences.i: “Good day, old mother,” said the king’s daughter, “what are you doing there?”
sentences.n: “Mütterchen” is an affectionate diminutive of Mutter, a courteous way to address an old woman; “du altes Mütterchen” is friendly, not literally her mother.

sentences.t: „Ich spinne,“ sagte die Alte und nickte mit dem Kopf.
sentences.l: “I am spinning,” said the old woman and nodded with her head.
sentences.i: “I am spinning,” said the old woman, nodding her head.
sentences.n: “die Alte” = ‘the old woman’ (adjective used as a noun). “nickte mit dem Kopf” — literally ‘nodded with the head,’ German names the body part with mit.

sentences.t: „Was ist das für ein Ding, das so lustig herumspringt?“ sprach das Mädchen, nahm die Spindel und wollte auch spinnen.
sentences.l: “What sort of thing is that, which springs about so merrily?” said the girl, took the spindle and wanted to spin too.
sentences.i: “What sort of thing is this that hops about so merrily?” said the girl, and she took the spindle and wanted to spin as well.
sentences.n: “Was für ein ...?” = ‘what sort of ...?’ “herumspringt” is separable herumspringen, ‘to jump/spin about.’

sentences.t: Kaum hatte sie aber die Spindel angerührt, so gieng der Zauberspruch in Erfüllung, und sie stach sich damit in den Finger.
sentences.l: But scarcely had she touched the spindle when the magic spell came to fulfilment, and she pricked herself with it in the finger.
sentences.i: But scarcely had she touched the spindle when the magic spell was fulfilled, and she pricked her finger with it.
sentences.n: “Kaum hatte sie ... so” = ‘scarcely had she ..., when’; the inverted verb-first order marks this ‘as soon as’ construction. “in Erfüllung gehen” = ‘to come true, be fulfilled.’ “stach sich in den Finger” — the dative reflexive shows whose finger: ‘pricked her(self in the) finger.’

sentences.t: In dem Augenblick aber, wo sie den Stich empfand, fiel sie auf das Bett nieder, das da stand, und lag in einem tiefen Schlaf.
sentences.l: But in the moment when she felt the prick, she fell down onto the bed that stood there, and lay in a deep sleep.
sentences.i: But at the very moment she felt the prick, she sank down upon the bed that stood there and lay in a deep sleep.
sentences.n: “fiel ... nieder” is separable niederfallen, ‘to fall down.’ “wo” here means ‘when’ (temporal), a common colloquial/older use after Augenblick.

sentences.t: Und dieser Schlaf verbreitete sich über das ganze Schloß: der König und die Königin, die eben heim gekommen waren und in den Saal getreten waren, fiengen an einzuschlafen, und der ganze Hofstaat mit ihnen.
sentences.l: And this sleep spread itself over the whole castle: the king and the queen, who had just come home and stepped into the hall, began to fall asleep, and the whole court with them.
sentences.i: And this sleep spread over the whole castle: the king and queen, who had just come home and entered the hall, began to fall asleep, and the whole court with them.
sentences.n: “fiengen an einzuschlafen” — anfangen (‘to begin’) plus zu + einschlafen; “fiengen ... an” is separable, and einzuschlafen is itself separable ein-. “Hofstaat” is the royal household/courtiers.

sentences.t: Da schliefen auch die Pferde im Stall, die Hunde im Hofe, die Tauben auf dem Dache, die Fliegen an der Wand, ja, das Feuer, das auf dem Herde flackerte, ward still und schlief ein, und der Braten hörte auf zu brutzeln, und der Koch, der den Küchenjungen, weil er etwas versehen hatte, in den Haaren ziehen wollte, ließ ihn los und schlief.
sentences.l: Then there also slept the horses in the stable, the dogs in the yard, the doves on the roof, the flies on the wall, yes, the fire that flickered on the hearth grew still and fell asleep, and the roast left off sizzling, and the cook, who wanted to pull the kitchen-boy by the hair because he had done something amiss, let him go and slept.
sentences.i: Then too the horses in the stable slept, the dogs in the yard, the doves on the roof, the flies on the wall—yes, even the fire flickering on the hearth grew still and fell asleep, the roast stopped sizzling, and the cook, who was about to pull the kitchen-boy’s hair because he had blundered, let him go and fell asleep.
sentences.n: A long list-sentence held together by repeated “und.” “schlief ein” (separable einschlafen) ‘fell asleep’; “hörte auf” (separable aufhören) ‘stopped’; “ließ ... los” (separable loslassen) ‘let go.’ “etwas versehen” — sich/etwas versehen, ‘to make a slip, blunder.’

sentences.t: Und der Wind legte sich, und auf den Bäumen vor dem Schloß regte sich kein Blättchen mehr.
sentences.l: And the wind laid itself down, and on the trees before the castle not a little leaf stirred any more.
sentences.i: And the wind died down, and on the trees before the castle not a single leaf stirred any longer.
sentences.n: “legte sich” — sich legen, of wind ‘to subside, die down.’ “regte sich” — sich regen, ‘to stir.’ “Blättchen” is the diminutive of Blatt, ‘little leaf.’

sentences.t: Rings um das Schloß aber begann eine Dornenhecke zu wachsen, die jedes Jahr höher ward, und endlich das ganze Schloß umzog, und darüber hinaus wuchs, daß gar nichts mehr davon zu sehen war, selbst nicht die Fahne auf dem Dach.
sentences.l: But round about the castle a hedge of thorns began to grow, which grew higher every year, and at last encircled the whole castle, and grew out beyond it, so that nothing at all of it could be seen any more, not even the flag on the roof.
sentences.i: But round about the castle a hedge of thorns began to grow, higher every year, until at last it encircled the whole castle and grew up over the top, so that nothing of it could be seen any longer, not even the banner on the roof.
sentences.n: “Rings um” = ‘all around.’ “zu sehen war” — sein + zu + infinitive expresses possibility/necessity passively: ‘was to be seen, could be seen.’ “darüber hinaus” = ‘out beyond, over the top.’

sentences.t: Es gieng aber die Sage in dem Land von dem schönen schlafenden Dornröschen, denn so ward die Königstochter genannt, also daß von Zeit zu Zeit Königssöhne kamen und durch die Hecke in das Schloß dringen wollten.
sentences.l: But the legend went about in the land of the beautiful sleeping Briar-Rose—for so was the king’s daughter called—so that from time to time kings’ sons came and wanted to force their way through the hedge into the castle.
sentences.i: But the tale went round the land of the beautiful sleeping Briar-Rose—for so the king’s daughter was called—so that from time to time kings’ sons came and tried to press their way through the hedge into the castle.
sentences.n: “die Sage gieng” = ‘the story/rumour went about.’ “Dornröschen” (Dorn ‘thorn’ + Röschen ‘little rose’) is the name given to the princess; kept untranslated as a name, glossed as ‘little briar-rose.’ “also daß” = ‘so that.’

sentences.t: Es war ihnen aber nicht möglich, denn die Dornen, als hätten sie Hände, hielten fest zusammen, und die Jünglinge blieben darin hängen, konnten sich nicht wieder los machen und starben eines jämmerlichen Todes.
sentences.l: But it was not possible for them, for the thorns, as though they had hands, held fast together, and the youths stayed caught in them, could not free themselves again, and died a wretched death.
sentences.i: But it was impossible for them, for the thorns, as if they had hands, clung fast together, and the young men were caught in them, could not get free again, and died a wretched death.
sentences.n: “als hätten sie Hände” is subjunctive II of comparison, ‘as if they had hands’ (verb-first after als). “blieben ... hängen” = ‘remained caught, stuck.’ “eines jämmerlichen Todes sterben” is the genitive of the death died: ‘to die a wretched death.’

sentences.t: Nach langen langen Jahren kam wieder einmal ein Königssohn in das Land, und hörte wie ein alter Mann von der Dornhecke erzählte, es sollte ein Schloß dahinter stehen, in welchem eine wunderschöne Königstochter, Dornröschen genannt, schon seit hundert Jahren schliefe, und mit ihr schliefe der König und die Königin und der ganze Hofstaat.
sentences.l: After long, long years, once again a king’s son came into the land, and heard how an old man told of the thorn-hedge, that a castle was said to stand behind it, in which a wondrously beautiful king’s daughter, called Briar-Rose, had already been sleeping for a hundred years, and with her slept the king and the queen and the whole court.
sentences.i: After long, long years a king’s son came into the land once more, and heard an old man tell of the thorn-hedge, that a castle was said to stand behind it in which a wondrously beautiful king’s daughter, called Briar-Rose, had slept for a hundred years already, and with her the king and queen and the whole court.
sentences.n: “es sollte ... stehen” — sollen here reports hearsay, ‘was said to.’ “schliefe” is subjunctive I of reported speech (‘was sleeping,’ as the old man tells it) — this reported-speech subjunctive runs all through Grimm. “seit hundert Jahren” with present-tense sense: ‘for a hundred years (and still).’

sentences.t: Er wußte auch von seinem Großvater daß schon viele Königssöhne gekommen wären und versucht hätten durch die Dornenhecke zu dringen, aber sie wären darin hängen geblieben und eines traurigen Todes gestorben.
sentences.l: He knew also from his grandfather that already many kings’ sons had come and had tried to press through the thorn-hedge, but they had stayed caught in it and had died a sorrowful death.
sentences.i: He knew too from his grandfather that many kings’ sons had already come and tried to force their way through the thorn-hedge, but had been caught in it and died a sorrowful death.
sentences.n: “wären ... gekommen,” “hätten ... versucht,” “wären ... geblieben,” “gestorben” are all subjunctive I/II of reported speech, carrying the grandfather’s report. “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste.

sentences.t: Da sprach der Jüngling „ich fürchte mich nicht, ich will hinaus und das schöne Dornröschen sehen.“
sentences.l: Then the youth said, “I am not afraid; I will go out and see the beautiful Briar-Rose.”
sentences.i: Then the young man said, “I am not afraid; I will go out there and see the beautiful Briar-Rose.”
sentences.n: “ich fürchte mich nicht” — sich fürchten, ‘to be afraid.’ “will hinaus” — wollen with the bare directional hinaus, the verb of motion (‘go’) left understood: ‘I want to (go) out.’

sentences.t: Der gute Alte mochte ihm abrathen, wie er wollte, er hörte nicht auf seine Worte.
sentences.l: The good old man might advise him against it however he would, he did not listen to his words.
sentences.i: However hard the good old man tried to dissuade him, he would not heed his words.
sentences.n: “mochte ... wie er wollte” is a concessive idiom: ‘however much he might ..., no matter how.’ “abrathen” (1857 spelling of abraten, separable ab-) = ‘to advise against, dissuade.’ “hörte nicht auf seine Worte” — auf jemanden/etwas hören, ‘to heed, listen to.’

sentences.t: Nun waren aber gerade die hundert Jahre verflossen, und der Tag war gekommen, wo Dornröschen wieder erwachen sollte.
sentences.l: But now the hundred years had just elapsed, and the day had come when Briar-Rose was to awaken again.
sentences.i: But now the hundred years had just run out, and the day had come when Briar-Rose was to wake once more.
sentences.n: “verflossen” (past part. of verfließen, of time ‘to elapse’). “erwachen sollte” — sollen for appointed destiny, ‘was to awaken.’ “wo” again = ‘when.’

sentences.t: Als der Königssohn sich der Dornenhecke näherte, waren es lauter große schöne Blumen, die thaten sich von selbst auseinander und ließen ihn unbeschädigt hindurch, und hinter ihm thaten sie sich wieder als eine Hecke zusammen.
sentences.l: When the king’s son approached the thorn-hedge, they were nothing but great beautiful flowers, which parted of themselves and let him through unharmed, and behind him they closed together again as a hedge.
sentences.i: When the king’s son drew near the thorn-hedge, they were nothing but great, lovely flowers, which parted of their own accord and let him pass through unharmed, and behind him they closed up again into a hedge.
sentences.n: “sich nähern” takes the dative: “näherte sich der Dornenhecke,” ‘approached the hedge.’ “lauter” = ‘nothing but, sheer.’ “thaten sich auseinander / zusammen” — sich auseinandertun / zusammentun, ‘to part / to close together’ (thaten is 1857 for taten). “von selbst” = ‘of its own accord.’

sentences.t: Im Schloßhof sah er die Pferde und scheckigen Jagdhunde liegen und schlafen, auf dem Dache saßen die Tauben und hatten das Köpfchen unter den Flügel gesteckt.
sentences.l: In the castle courtyard he saw the horses and dappled hunting hounds lying and sleeping; on the roof sat the doves and had tucked their little heads under their wings.
sentences.i: In the castle courtyard he saw the horses and the dappled hunting hounds lying asleep; on the roof the doves sat with their little heads tucked under their wings.
sentences.n: “sah ... liegen und schlafen” — perception verb sehen + bare infinitives, ‘saw (them) lie and sleep.’ “Köpfchen” is the diminutive of Kopf; “hatten ... gesteckt” is pluperfect of stecken, ‘had tucked.’

sentences.t: Und als er ins Haus kam, schliefen die Fliegen an der Wand, der Koch in der Küche hielt noch die Hand, als wollte er den Jungen anpacken, und die Magd saß vor dem schwarzen Huhn, das sollte gerupft werden.
sentences.l: And when he came into the house, the flies slept on the wall, the cook in the kitchen still held his hand as if he wanted to grab the boy, and the maid sat before the black hen that was to be plucked.
sentences.i: And when he came into the house, the flies slept on the wall; the cook in the kitchen still held out his hand as if to seize the boy, and the maid sat before the black hen that was to be plucked.
sentences.n: “als wollte er ... anpacken” is subjunctive of comparison, ‘as if he wanted to grab’ (anpacken, separable an-). “das sollte gerupft werden” — passive with sollen, ‘which was to be plucked.’

sentences.t: Da gieng er weiter, und sah im Saale den ganzen Hofstaat liegen und schlafen, und oben bei dem Throne lag der König und die Königin.
sentences.l: Then he went on further, and saw in the hall the whole court lying and sleeping, and up by the throne lay the king and the queen.
sentences.i: Then he went on further, and saw in the hall the whole court lying asleep, and up beside the throne lay the king and the queen.
sentences.n: “gieng ... weiter” — separable weitergehen, ‘to go on.’ “Throne” keeps the old dative -e (bei dem Throne).

sentences.t: Da gieng er noch weiter, und alles war so still, daß einer seinen Athem hören konnte, und endlich kam er zu dem Thurm und öffnete die Thüre zu der kleinen Stube, in welcher Dornröschen schlief.
sentences.l: Then he went still further, and all was so still that one could hear one’s own breath, and at last he came to the tower and opened the door to the little room in which Briar-Rose slept.
sentences.i: Then he went further still, and everything was so quiet that one could hear one’s own breathing, and at last he came to the tower and opened the door of the little room where Briar-Rose slept.
sentences.n: “einer ... seinen Athem” — einer is the indefinite ‘one,’ and seinen refers back to it: ‘one’s own breath’ (Athem is the 1857 spelling of Atem).

sentences.t: Da lag es und war so schön, daß er die Augen nicht abwenden konnte, und er bückte sich und gab ihm einen Kuß.
sentences.l: There she lay and was so beautiful that he could not turn his eyes away, and he bent down and gave her a kiss.
sentences.i: There she lay, so beautiful that he could not take his eyes off her, and he bent down and gave her a kiss.
sentences.n: The neuter “es” / “ihm” still refers to das Mädchen / Dornröschen (grammatical gender). “die Augen abwenden” = ‘to turn one’s eyes away.’ “bückte sich” — sich bücken, ‘to stoop, bend down.’

sentences.t: Wie er es mit dem Kuß berührt hatte, schlug Dornröschen die Augen auf, erwachte, und blickte ihn ganz freundlich an.
sentences.l: As he had touched her with the kiss, Briar-Rose struck her eyes open, awoke, and looked at him quite kindly.
sentences.i: The moment he had touched her with the kiss, Briar-Rose opened her eyes, awoke, and looked at him very kindly.
sentences.n: “Wie” here = ‘as, the moment.’ “schlug ... auf” is separable aufschlagen; “die Augen aufschlagen” = ‘to open the eyes.’ “blickte ... an” is separable anblicken, ‘to look at.’

sentences.t: Da giengen sie zusammen herab, und der König erwachte und die Königin, und der ganze Hofstaat, und sahen einander mit großen Augen an.
sentences.l: Then they went down together, and the king awoke and the queen, and the whole court, and looked at one another with great eyes.
sentences.i: Then they went down together, and the king and the queen awoke, and the whole court, and they gazed at one another with wide eyes.
sentences.n: “giengen ... herab” — separable herabgehen, ‘to go down.’ “sahen einander ... an” — ansehen with the reciprocal einander, ‘looked at one another.’

sentences.t: Und die Pferde im Hof standen auf und rüttelten sich: die Jagdhunde sprangen und wedelten: die Tauben auf dem Dache zogen das Köpfchen unterm Flügel hervor, sahen umher und flogen ins Feld: die Fliegen an den Wänden krochen weiter: das Feuer in der Küche erhob sich, flackerte: und kochte das Essen: der Braten fieng wieder an zu brutzeln: und der Koch gab dem Jungen eine Ohrfeige daß er schrie: und die Magd rupfte das Huhn fertig.
sentences.l: And the horses in the yard stood up and shook themselves; the hunting hounds sprang and wagged their tails; the doves on the roof drew their little heads out from under their wings, looked about, and flew into the field; the flies on the walls crept on; the fire in the kitchen rose up, flickered, and cooked the food; the roast began to sizzle again; and the cook gave the boy such a box on the ear that he cried out; and the maid finished plucking the hen.
sentences.i: And the horses in the yard stood up and shook themselves; the hounds leapt and wagged their tails; the doves on the roof drew their little heads from under their wings, looked about, and flew off into the fields; the flies on the walls crept on; the fire in the kitchen flared up and cooked the food; the roast began to sizzle again; the cook gave the boy such a box on the ear that he yelled; and the maid finished plucking the hen.
sentences.n: A rush of re-awakening in one sentence, the clauses separated by colons in the 1857 print. Note the parade of separable verbs: “standen auf” (aufstehen), “zogen ... hervor” (hervorziehen), “erhob sich” (sich erheben), “fieng ... an” (anfangen). “rüttelten sich” — sich rütteln, ‘to shake oneself.’ “unterm” = unter dem.

sentences.t: Und da wurde die Hochzeit des Königssohns mit dem Dornröschen in aller Pracht gefeiert, und sie lebten vergnügt bis an ihr Ende.
sentences.l: And then the wedding of the king’s son with Briar-Rose was celebrated with all splendour, and they lived contentedly until their end.
sentences.i: And so the wedding of the king’s son to Briar-Rose was celebrated with all magnificence, and they lived happily until their end.
sentences.n: “bis an ihr Ende” = ‘to their end’ — the closing formula, here ‘happily ever after.’ Note the use of modern “wurde” here (rather than ward) even in this 1857 text.
